title: Mat-Su to distribute millions in opioid help under new borough-city partnerships
summary: Matanuska-Susitna Borough Administration Building in Palmer (Photo by Amy Bushatz/Mat-Su Sentinel) PALMER — Mat-Su officials will distribute millions in opioid addiction relief funding under a new partnership between the region’s cities and the borough approved by the Assembly last week. The partnership allows officials to use more than $2 million that the borough will receive from a national opioid settlement to pay for a...

title: AK Defense Forum, Events, and Energy Ratings
summary: Hey Neighbors, Summers in the Interior are short, beautiful and packed with barbeques for family and friends, fishing, attending the Midnight Sun Run activities, watching Gold Panner baseball games and making trips to the Salmon Bake and the Tanana Valley State Fair. My legislative office was also q...

title: Governor Dunleavy Announces Agreement With Federal Permitting Council to Streamline Infrastructure Development
summary: Governor Mike Dunleavy today announced that the State of Alaska has signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council to improve coordination and transparency in permitting major infrastructure projects across the state. The MOU is the product of conversations initiated at th...

title: Talk on the Rock: KSTK’s general manager gives an update on how the station is affected from the clawback of federal funds
summary: KSTK’s Cindy Sweat informs the community on Aug. 26, 2025 about changes the station has been making since Congress defunded federal funds for public broadcasting. (Colette Czarnecki/KSTK) Episode 65: Last month we had KSTK’s General Manager Cindy Sweat on air talking about the threat of public media...

title: Tribal radio stations wait on $9M pledged in congressional handshake deal
summary: U.S. Sen. Mike Rounds (center) and tribal leaders speak to the media after a public safety roundtable on Aug. 14, 2024, in Wagner, South Dakota. With Rounds, from left, are Cheyenne River Chairman Ryman LeBeau, Lower Brule Chairman Clyde Estes, Sisseton Wahpeton Secretary Curtis Bissonette, Wayne Boyd of Rosebud Sioux Tribe, Yankton Chairman Robert Flying Hawk, Oglala President Frank Star Comes out and Crow Creek Chairman Peter Lengkeek. (Photo by Makenzie Huber/South Dakota Searchlight) WASHINGTON — Tribal radio stations that are supposed to receive millions to fill the hole created when Congress eliminated funding for the Corporation for Public Broadcasting haven’t heard anything from the Trump administration about when it will send the money or how m...

title: AST REDDI: Delta Junction [AK25085074]
summary: On August 22, 2025, at approximately 2135 hours, AST received a REDDI report regarding an off-road vehicle in the area of Delta Junction. AST located the vehicle and initiated a traffic stop. The vehicle failed to stop for AST and evaded. The investigation is ongoing.

title: Trump Administration Moves to Roll Back Popular Roadless Rule, Threatening Alaska’s Tongass, Chugach, and National Forests Nationwide
summary: FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE Date: August 27, 2025 Contact : Anja Semanco | 724-967-2777 | anja@alaskawild.org Trump Administration Moves to Roll Back Popular Roadless Rule, Threatening Alaska’s Tongass, Chugach, and National Forests Nationwide WASHINGTON, D.C. – In a sweeping attack on America’s public lands, the U.S. Department of Agriculture announced...

title: KHNS Question Corner – Fall Harvesting and Preserving – August 28th 10am
summary: As summer wanes and fall is on the horizon, what are Upper Lynn Canal residents harvesting and foraging? How are they preserving their bounty? Join chef and forager Travis Kukull, Tlingit Culture Bearer Lani Hotch, and other guests as they answer these questions and more on KHNS’s Question Corner, t...

title: Wednesday, August 27, 2025
summary: Photo: The Ukpeaġvik Iñupiat Corporation office in Utqiagvik. (Courtesy Ukpeaġvik Iñupiat Corporation) The federal government is in the process of rewriting the rules for how it issues federal contracts and some Alaska Native organizations, which depend on federal contracts to pay out benefits to shareholders, are concerned that it could impact their business. The Alaska Desk’s Alena Naiden from our flagship station KNBA has more. Christo...

title: U.S. House delegation visits Alaska this week, with focus on mining, timber and drilling
summary: Members of the U.S. House Committee on Natural Resoures talk with reporters at Juneau International Airport on Monday, Aug. 25, 2025. (Photo by James Brooks/Alaska Beacon) Ten members of the U.S. House of Representatives’ Committee on Natural Resources are making an unusual visit to Alaska this week during a break from business on Capitol Hill. The 45-person committee deals with a variety of issues pertaining to public lands in the United States, and the visit...

title: New University of Alaska Fairbanks chancellor Mike Sfraga brings focus and enthusiasm for ‘America’s Arctic university’
summary: This story was originally published by the Alaska Beacon Corinne Smith Alaska Beacon The former first U.S. ambassador-at-large to the Arctic, Mike Sfraga, has stepped into a new role as interim chancellor of the University of Alaska Fairbanks, and brings experience, focus, and a unique passion for the state’s leading research institution, which describes itself as “America’s Arctic university.” In a phone interview in early August, Sfraga talked about his goals and challenges in the role, which is expected to last one year, as the University of Alaska continues a search for a permanent chancellor. “Well, a number of things drew me to this role. One is, it’s my alma mater,” he said. Sfraga graduated from UAF in 1997 from the first doctoral program in Northern studies and geography. “I believe in its mission for the state,” Sfraga said. “...

title: As Alaska Legislature convenes education task force, lawmakers have different views of where to go
summary: Students begin their first day of school at the Tlingit Culture, Language and Literacy program at Harborview Elementary School in Juneau on Aug. 15, 2025 (Photo by Corinne Smith/Alaska Beacon) The Alaska Legislature opened an 18-month study of the state’s troubled public education system on Tuesday as lawmakers convened the first meeting of their Task Force on Education Funding, established by law this spring.
